attribution.note: Legendary Greek fabulist — original tales from oral tradition

name: Aesop's Fables for Little Ones — Storyboard
description:
Production storyboard for animated series based on 30 of Aesop's most beloved fables. Each fable is a 5-minute episode split into 3 scenes with rhyming narration, AI image prompts, and camera directions. Designed for AI-animated episodes in the style of Mati and Dada. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #21 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/21).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Arthur Rackham's 1912 illustrations for 'Aesop's Fables' (Heinemann) — pen-and-ink drawings with watercolor. Milo Winter's 1919 illustrations for 'The Aesop for Children' (Rand McNally) — full-color. Walter Crane's 1887 'The Baby's Own Aesop' — woodcut-style. Ernest Griset's 1869 illustrations (Cassell) — detailed pen-and-ink.

items.name: Ep1 Scene 1: The Tortoise and the Hare

sections.Narration:
A Hare once BRAGGED to all the wood,
"I'm faster than you EVER could!
No creature here can match my feet —
I'm quicker than the summer HEAT!"

A Tortoise smiled and raised her head:
"I'll race you to the hill," she said.
The animals all gasped, "No WAY!"
But Tortoise said, "I'll race TODAY."

— Aesop's Fables, trans. V.S. Vernon Jones (1912) —

items.name: Ep1 Scene 2: The Tortoise and the Hare

sections.Narration:
The Fox said, "GO!" — the Hare TOOK OFF,
So fast the dust made everyone cough!
But halfway there he stopped to rest:
"I've TIME," he yawned. "I am the BEST."

He stretched beneath a shady tree,
And closed his eyes so lazily.
Meanwhile the Tortoise, step by STEP,
Just kept on walking while Hare SLEPT.

items.name: Ep1 Scene 3: The Tortoise and the Hare

sections.Narration:
The Hare woke up — "Oh no! Oh DEAR!"
The Tortoise was already HERE!
She crossed the line, the crowd went WILD,
And every single creature SMILED.

Slow and steady, step by step,
Wins the race while others slept.
It's not about who's fast or STRONG —
It's those who keep on going LONG.

items.name: Ep2 Scene 1: The Fox and the Grapes

sections.Narration:
A Fox was walking, belly BARE,
When something lovely caught the AIR —
Sweet purple grapes on vines so HIGH,
They dangled from the summer SKY!

"Oh MY!" said Fox, "what gorgeous FRUIT!
Those grapes would really, truly SUIT
My rumbling tummy — what a TREAT!"
And Fox began to move his FEET.

sections.Narration:
He jumped up HIGH! He jumped up TALL!
He bounced and leaped against the WALL!
He tried and tried with all his MIGHT,
But couldn't reach — not LEFT or RIGHT.

He stretched his paws, he stood on TOES,
He even tried to use his NOSE!
But every time he'd jump and MISS,
The grapes stayed high above his WISH.

items.name: Ep2 Scene 3: The Fox and the Grapes

sections.Narration:
At last the Fox walked off with PRIDE,
His nose tipped up, his chin held WIDE:
"Those grapes are SOUR anyway,
I wouldn't eat them ANY day!"

But were they sour? We'll never KNOW.
He couldn't reach them, that's the SHOW.
When you can't get what you DESIRE,
Don't call the grapes a stinky LIAR!

items.name: Ep3 Scene 1: The Lion and the Mouse

sections.Narration:
A Lion slept beneath a TREE,
As peaceful as a cat could BE,
When — SCURRY SCAMPER — what was THIS?
A tiny Mouse stole one small KISS!

She ran across his golden MANE!
The Lion ROARED: "You're quite INSANE!"
He caught the Mouse beneath his PAW:
"I'll eat you up!" he said with AWE.

sections.SourceQuote:
— Aesop's Fables, trans. V.S. Vernon Jones (1912) —

"A Lion asleep in his lair was waked up by a Mouse running over his face. Losing his temper he seized it with his paw and was about to kill it. The Mouse, terrified, piteously entreated him to spare its life. 'Please let me go,' it cried, 'and one day I will repay you for your kindness.'"

items.name: Ep3 Scene 2: The Lion and the Mouse

sections.Narration:
"Oh please!" the Mouse cried, "Let me GO!
I'm small, but someday you will KNOW —
I'll help you back, I PROMISE true!"
The Lion laughed: "What could YOU do?"

But still, he opened up his PAW.
"Go on, small thing," he said with THAW.
The Mouse ran off — SQUEAK, SQUEAK, hooray!
She'd live to see another DAY.

items.name: Ep3 Scene 3: The Lion and the Mouse

sections.Narration:
But days went by and Lion got CAUGHT
In hunters' ropes — all tied and TAUT!
He roared and thrashed but couldn't BREAK FREE,
Till Mouse appeared beside his KNEE.

"I'm HERE!" she squeaked, and GNAWED the rope,
And gave that mighty Lion HOPE.
The smallest friend can save the DAY —
Be kind to ALL who cross your WAY.

items.name: Ep4 Scene 1: The Ant and the Grasshopper

sections.Narration:
All summer long the Ants worked HARD,
They carried crumbs across the YARD,
They stored up food for winter DAYS,
And sang their busy working PRAISE.

But Grasshopper just played his SONG,
And danced and hopped the whole day LONG:
"Why WORK?" he laughed, "The sun is BRIGHT!
There's time for food — let's dance TONIGHT!"

sections.Narration:
Then WINTER came with ice and SNOW,
The cold north wind began to BLOW.
The Ants were warm inside their NEST,
With food to eat and time to REST.

But Grasshopper shivered in the COLD,
His fiddle silent, songs all TOLD.
He knocked upon the ants' front DOOR:
"Please help me! I don't have a STORE!"

items.name: Ep4 Scene 3: The Ant and the Grasshopper

sections.Narration:
The Ants looked out: "What did you DO
All summer long? We worked — did YOU?"
"I sang," said Grasshopper, head hung LOW.
"Then DANCE through winter in the snow!"

But here's the thing that's also TRUE:
Work hard, but make some music TOO.
The ant needs song, the bug needs BREAD —
The wisest ones do BOTH instead.

items.name: Ep5 Scene 1: The Boy Who Cried Wolf

sections.Narration:
A shepherd boy sat on a HILL,
His sheep were grazing, calm and STILL.
But oh, the boy was SO much BORED!
He wanted FUN — he wanted MORE!

"I KNOW!" he grinned, "I'll play a TRICK!"
He jumped right up, so sly and SLICK,
And shouted loud for all to HEAR:
"WOLF! WOLF! A WOLF is coming HERE!"

items.name: Ep5 Scene 2: The Boy Who Cried Wolf

sections.Narration:
The villagers came running FAST,
With pitchforks, sticks — they held on FAST!
But when they got there — what'd they FIND?

He sold each egg for bags of GOLD,
And soon had riches, truth be TOLD.

Unless..." — he found a sheepskin WHITE —
"I'll DRESS like them and sneak in TIGHT!"

They argued over everything BOUGHT.

And NEVER could they work things OUT.

"Come here, my boys," the old man SAID.
He brought a bundle, tightly BOUND,
Of sticks he'd gathered from the GROUND.

Borrowed beauty leaves you ALL alone.
Be proud of who you truly ARE —
A genuine crow beats a fake STAR.

items.name: Ep24 Scene 1: The Tortoise and the Eagle

sections.Narration:
A Tortoise watched the eagles SOAR
And wished that she could fly and MORE.
"Oh Eagle!" Tortoise called up HIGH,
"PLEASE teach me how to touch the SKY!"

"You CAN'T," said Eagle. "You're a SHELL.
You're built for ground — and quite well, WELL."
"But I don't WANT the ground!" she CRIED,
"I want to fly! I want to GLIDE!"

And as the Tortoise looked DOWN there,
She saw the rocks below — OH BEAR!

Her shell was cracked like broken CLOCKS.

She whispered softly through her SONG.

Wish for wings but know your GROUND —
Your roots are where your strength is FOUND.
A tortoise doesn't need to FLY
To find the beauty of the SKY.

Two Goats met on a bridge one DAY —
A tiny bridge across the WAY.
It hung above a river DEEP,
So narrow only ONE could CREEP.

"Move OVER!" said the first goat, CROSS.
"No, YOU move!" — neither liked a LOSS.
They stamped their hooves, they shook their BEARD,
And neither one of them had FEARED.

They BOTH would have been on their WAY.
Sometimes the strongest thing to DO
Is step aside and let things THROUGH.

A Heron stood beside a STREAM
On one long leg, as in a DREAM.
The fish swam by in silver SCHOOLS —
Fat trout and perch and little FOOLS.

"Hmph," said Heron, looking DOWN,
"Those fish are small — I'll wear a FROWN.
I deserve BETTER fish than THESE.
I'll wait for something sure to PLEASE."

Convinced that something grand would CALL.
But as the sun began to SET,

At last, when twilight filled the AIR,
A tiny snail was ALL that's there.
The hungry Heron ate it QUICK —
No choosing now — just take the PICK.

Don't turn your beak up at what's GOOD
While waiting for the PERFECT food.
A fish in beak is worth much MORE
Than ten imagined fish onshore.

And lost his tail — oh, what a SNAP!
He pulled and tugged and got away,
But left his fluffy tail that DAY.

Without my tail I'll be the BLUSH
Of every fox who sees me HERE —
They'll laugh at me from ear to EAR!"

"I'll tell them tails are NOT the jam!"
He called a meeting — every FOX
Came round to hear him, sly as CLOCKS.

Tails are USELESS! They're uncouth!
Cut them OFF! You'll run more FAST!
Tails are nothing but the PAST!"

items.name: Episode 1: The Tortoise and the Hare

sections.About: Episode 1 of the series. 3 scenes, ~80 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: A Hare once BRAGGED to all the wood,... → The Fox said, "GO!" — the Hare TOOK OFF,... → The Hare woke up — "Oh no! Oh DEAR!"...
sections.Wonder: Have you ever been in a race? Is it always the fastest person who wins? What other things matter besides speed?
sections.Whisper: This fable — perhaps the most universally known of Aesop's — encodes the paradox of wu wei: the tortoise wins not through effort but through consistent presence. In Taoist terms, she embodies the watercourse way — not forcing, simply persisting.

sections.About: Episode 2 of the series. 3 scenes, ~81 seconds total narration.

sections.Wonder: Have you ever said something was 'stupid' or 'boring' just because you couldn't have it? What were the grapes really like?
sections.Whisper: Freud named an entire defense mechanism after this fable: 'sour grapes' rationalization. The fox's cognitive dissonance — desiring what he cannot reach, then devaluing it — is a universal human pattern that Aesop identified 2,500 years before psychoanalysis.

sections.About: Episode 10 of the series. 3 scenes, ~70 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: A Dog once found a juicy BONE,... → Down in the water, clear as GLASS,... → He BARKED! He snapped! He opened WIDE —...
sections.Wonder: Have you ever wanted something so badly that you lost what you already had? What's the difference between real things and reflections?
sections.Whisper: Plato would have recognized this fable immediately — the dog mistakes shadow for substance, pursuing the reflection (eidos) rather than the real (ousia). It's a folk philosophy of the Cave allegory, accessible to a five-year-old.

items.name: Episode 11: The Goose That Laid the Golden Eggs

sections.About: Episode 11 of the series. 3 scenes, ~72 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: A farmer had a special GOOSE,... → But farmer grew more greedy STILL:... → He cut the goose — but what was THIS?...
sections.Wonder: If you had a magic goose, would you be patient? What does 'enough' look like to you?
sections.Whisper: This fable is the ur-text of sustainable economics. The farmer's error — destroying a renewable resource for a one-time extraction — is the logic of strip mining, deforestation, and financial bubbles. Aesop was the first ecological economist.

items.name: Episode 12: The Wolf in Sheep's Clothing

sections.About: Episode 12 of the series. 3 scenes, ~76 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: A Wolf was hungry, thin and LEAN,... → He pulled the fleece over his HEAD,... → But THEN that night the shepherd CAME...
sections.Wonder: Can you always tell who is good and who is pretending? What clues help you figure out who someone really is?
sections.Whisper: Jesus references this fable directly in Matthew 7:15: 'Beware of false prophets, who come to you in sheep's clothing.' The image has become so embedded in language that we forget its Aesopic origin. The wolf's disguise is the archetype of all deception.

items.name: Episode 13: The Bundle of Sticks

sections.About: Episode 13 of the series. 3 scenes, ~72 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: A father had three sons who FOUGHT,... → "Now BREAK this bundle — use your MIGHT!"... → The father smiled and UNTIED the bunch,...
sections.Wonder: Do you and your siblings or friends ever fight? What happens when you work together instead? Can you think of a time teamwork made something easier?
sections.Whisper: This fable has been adopted by movements from Roman fasces (the bundle of rods symbolizing collective strength) to modern trade unions. Its message — that divided entities fall to forces they could collectively resist — is the foundation of solidarity politics.

items.name: Episode 14: The Milkmaid and Her Pail

sections.About: Episode 14 of the series. 3 scenes, ~75 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: A Milkmaid walked along the LANE,... → "I'll sell the eggs for SO much gold,... → She tossed her head — just like her DREAM —...
sections.Wonder: Do you ever daydream about what you'll do with something before you have it? What happens when you get too far ahead of yourself?
sections.Whisper: The milkmaid's cascade of imagined wealth — milk → eggs → chickens → dress → admiration — is the exact structure of what behavioral economists call 'planning fallacy.' Each assumption compounds, building a castle of probability on a foundation of fantasy.

items.name: Episode 15: The Oak and the Reed

sections.About: Episode 15 of the series. 3 scenes, ~75 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: A mighty Oak stood tall and PROUD,... → Then came a STORM — the thunder CRASHED!... → The storm passed by — the Reed stood UP,...
sections.Wonder: Is it better to be strong and stiff, or soft and bendy? When is it good to be flexible?
sections.Whisper: This fable directly parallels Lao Tzu's observation that 'the stiff and unbending is the disciple of death; the soft and yielding is the disciple of life.' Whether Aesop influenced Eastern philosophy or both drew from common human observation remains debated.

items.name: Episode 16: The Dog in the Manger

sections.About: Episode 16 of the series. 3 scenes, ~77 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: A Dog climbed into a manger HIGH,... → Along came Ox, so tired and THIN:... → The animals all walked AWAY,...
sections.Wonder: Have you ever not wanted something but also didn't want anyone else to have it? Why do we sometimes act like that?
sections.Whisper: The phrase 'dog in the manger' entered English as a proverb for anyone who prevents others from enjoying what they themselves cannot use. It's a precise diagnosis of spite — the irrational preference for mutual deprivation over another's gain.

items.name: Episode 17: The Fox and the Stork

sections.About: Episode 17 of the series. 3 scenes, ~76 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: The Fox invited Stork to DINE:... → But Stork was clever — yes INDEED!... → "How's dinner, Fox?" asked Stork with GRIN,...
sections.Wonder: How does it feel when someone tricks you? Is it okay to trick them back? What's a better way to handle it?
sections.Whisper: This fable is Aesop's version of the Golden Rule rendered in negative form — 'what you do to others will be done to you.' The reciprocal dinner invitations create a perfect mirror structure that makes the moral inescapable.

items.name: Episode 18: Belling the Cat

sections.About: Episode 18 of the series. 3 scenes, ~75 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: The Mice held council late one NIGHT:... → A young mouse jumped up: "HERE'S my thought!... → But then the old mouse cleared his THROAT:...
sections.Wonder: Have you ever had a great idea but nobody wanted to do it? What's the difference between having an idea and actually doing it?
sections.Whisper: This fable gave English the phrase 'who will bell the cat?' — meaning who will undertake a dangerous task for the common good. It's the foundational text on the gap between strategy and execution, between committee wisdom and individual courage.

items.name: Episode 19: The Frog and the Ox

sections.About: Episode 19 of the series. 3 scenes, ~73 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: A little Frog saw something BIG —... → "BIGGER!" said the baby FROG.... → And then — oh NO! — she puffed too FAR!...
sections.Wonder: Have you ever tried to make yourself look bigger or more important than you are? What happened?
sections.Whisper: The frog's fatal inflation is Aesop's commentary on hubris — the Greek concept of overreaching one's natural bounds. The physical impossibility (a frog cannot become an ox) mirrors the psychological impossibility of becoming what one fundamentally is not.

items.name: Episode 20: The Ass in the Lion's Skin

sections.About: Episode 20 of the series. 3 scenes, ~71 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: An Ass once found a Lion's SKIN... → He strutted through the forest WIDE,... → The Fox just laughed: "I KNOW that sound!...
sections.Wonder: What happens when you pretend to be something you're not? Do people eventually find out? Is it better to just be yourself?
sections.Whisper: The donkey's bray — the involuntary eruption of authentic self through artificial disguise — anticipates what psychologists call 'leakage': the way true nature inevitably reveals itself through unguarded moments, no matter how careful the performance.

items.name: Episode 21: The Farmer and the Snake

sections.About: Episode 21 of the series. 3 scenes, ~75 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: One winter day, so cold and GRAY,... → He tucked the Snake beneath his COAT,... → The Snake reared up and — HISS! — it BIT!...
sections.Wonder: Should you help everyone, even if they might hurt you? How do you know when to be careful?
sections.Whisper: This dark fable raises uncomfortable questions about essential nature versus circumstance. Is the snake evil, or simply being a snake? Aesop leaves this unresolved — a moral ambiguity rare in fable literature that has fueled philosophical debate for millennia.

sections.About: Episode 22 of the series. 3 scenes, ~70 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: The Peacock spread his feathers WIDE —... → He went to Juno, queen of SKIES:... → "The eagle has his soaring FLIGHT,...
sections.Wonder: What's YOUR special gift? What can you do that nobody else can do quite the same way?
sections.Whisper: Juno's wisdom — that each creature has a unique gift — anticipates Howard Gardner's theory of multiple intelligences by 2,500 years. The peacock's error is comparing himself on the nightingale's axis rather than celebrating his own.

items.name: Episode 23: The Crow and the Peacock Feathers

sections.About: Episode 23 of the series. 3 scenes, ~76 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: A plain black Crow looked at the GROUND... → She walked among the peacock CROWD:... → She flew back to the other CROWS,...
sections.Wonder: Have you ever tried to copy someone else instead of being yourself? Did it work out? What makes YOU special?
sections.Whisper: The crow's double rejection — by both peacocks AND crows — is Aesop's warning about cultural displacement. Those who abandon their authentic community for an aspirational one may find themselves belonging to neither. It's a fable about the cost of inauthenticity.

items.name: Episode 24: The Tortoise and the Eagle

sections.About: Episode 24 of the series. 3 scenes, ~78 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: A Tortoise watched the eagles SOAR... → The Eagle sighed and picked her UP,... → Down she fell — CRASH! — upon the ROCKS....
sections.Wonder: What can't you do that you wish you could? Is that okay? What CAN you do really well instead?
sections.Whisper: This fable inverts the modern 'you can be anything' narrative with Aesop's older wisdom: some limitations are structural, not motivational. The tortoise's shell — her defining feature — is precisely what prevents flight. Strength and limitation are often the same thing.

items.name: Episode 25: The Two Goats

sections.About: Episode 25 of the series. 3 scenes, ~74 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: Two Goats met on a bridge one DAY —... → "I was here FIRST!" "No, I was ME!"... → And THEN — you guessed — they BOTH fell IN!...
sections.Wonder: Have you ever had a standoff with someone where neither of you would back down? What happened? What would have been smarter?
sections.Whisper: Game theorists would recognize this as a pure 'chicken game' — two agents heading for mutual destruction, where the rational move (yielding) feels like weakness. Aesop demonstrates that mutual stubbornness produces the worst possible outcome for both parties.

items.name: Episode 26: The Heron

sections.About: Episode 26 of the series. 3 scenes, ~70 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: A Heron stood beside a STREAM... → The big fish swam away downstream,... → At last, when twilight filled the AIR,...
sections.Wonder: Have you ever been too picky and missed out on something good? Is it okay to just enjoy what's there?
sections.Whisper: The heron's escalating rejection mirrors what psychologists call the 'paradox of choice' — when abundant options lead not to satisfaction but to paralysis and regret. Barry Schwartz's modern research on maximizers vs. satisficers echoes this 2,500-year-old fable.

items.name: Episode 27: The Fox Who Lost His Tail

sections.About: Episode 27 of the series. 3 scenes, ~76 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: A Fox got caught inside a TRAP,... → He thought and thought, then had a PLAN:... → An old fox smiled and said, "My DEAR,...
sections.Wonder: Has anyone ever told you to do something that was really just good for THEM? How can you tell when advice is selfish?
sections.Whisper: This fable is the earliest known description of what psychologists call 'projection' and 'universalization of loss.' The fox doesn't just cope with his loss — he attempts to normalize it, transforming personal misfortune into universal philosophy. It's advice-giving as defense mechanism.

items.name: Episode 28: The Lion's Share

sections.About: Episode 28 of the series. 3 scenes, ~70 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: The Lion, Fox, and Donkey went... → The Donkey split it — one, two, THREE —... → The Fox stepped up, all shrewd and SLY,...
sections.Wonder: Is it always fair to share equally? What happens when the most powerful person takes the most? Is that okay?
sections.Whisper: The phrase 'the lion's share' — meaning the largest portion, taken by the most powerful — comes directly from this fable. It's Aesop's most explicitly political tale: a meditation on how power distorts fairness, and how the weak must navigate systems built by the strong.

sections.Arc: Scenes: The Mice were losing every FIGHT... → The battle came — the Weasels WON!... → The helmets jammed! The plumes got STUCK!...
sections.Wonder: Have you ever seen someone who was so fancy they couldn't do their job? Is it better to look good or to be good at something?
sections.Whisper: Military historians cite this fable in discussions of 'officer culture' — when leaders become so invested in the trappings of authority that they lose the agility of those they command. The generals' plumes are decorative rather than functional — status symbols that become lethal liabilities.

items.name: Episode 30: The Two Travelers and the Bear

sections.About: Episode 30 of the series. 3 scenes, ~74 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: Two friends went walking through the WOOD,... → Then — CRACK! A Bear came through the TREES!... → The Bear sniffed close and went AWAY....
sections.Wonder: How can you tell who your real friends are? Is it when things are easy, or when things are hard?
sections.Whisper: This fable gives us the proverb 'a friend in need is a friend indeed' — perhaps the most widely quoted of all Aesopic morals. The bear functions as a truth-revealing device: danger strips away social performance and reveals the authentic quality of relationships.
